title: Games
text: Home * Games John von Neumann classified Chess as two-player zero-sum abstract strategy board game with perfect information . This page gives a brief overview on chess variants and other games, which are interesting for chess programmers due to new and innovative approaches and algorithms in search , evaluation and learning . Contents 1 Board Games 1.1 Chess Variants 1.2 Abstract Board Games 1.3 Mancala Games 1.4 Games of Chance 1.5 Without perfect information 2 Card Games 3 Word Games 4 Single-player 5 Misc 6 See also 7 Publications 7.1 1913 7.2 1920 ... 7.3 1930 ... 7.4 1940 ... 7.5 1950 ... 7.6 1960 ... 7.7 1970 ... 7.8 1980 ... 7.9 1990 ... 7.10 1995 ... 7.11 2000 ... 7.12 2005 ... 7.13 2010 ... 7.14 2015 ... 7.15 2020 ... 8 Forum Posts 8.1 1990 ... 8.2 2000 ... 8.3 2010 ... 8.4 2015 ... 8.5 2020 ... 9 External Links 9.1 Type of Games 9.2 Sport 9.2.1 Cue sports 9.2.2 Robot sports 9.3 Game theory 9.4 Complexity 9.5 Solving 9.6 Player 9.7 Agents 9.8 Games Scholars 9.9 Workshops & Events 9.9.1 Ingo Althöfer 9.9.2 Game Theory 9.10 Organizations 9.11 Online 9.12 Blogs 9.13 Misc 10 References Board Games [ edit ] Chess Variants [ edit ] Antichess (Losing Chess) Atomic Chess Capablanca Chess Chess960 or Fischer Random Chess (FRC) Chinese Chess (Xiangqi) Crazyhouse Gothic Chess Kinglet Knightmate Chess Losing Chess Nightrider Chess Seirawan Chess [1] Shatranj Shogi (Japanese Chess) Shuffle Chess Suicide Chess (Losing Chess) Xiangqi (Chinese Chess) Abstract Board Games [ edit ] Abalone Amazons Arimaa Ataxx Breakthrough Checkers Clobber Connect Four Connect6 Draughts Go Gomoku Havannah Hex Lines of Action Nine Men’s Morris Othello Renju Mancala Games [ edit ] Awari Bao Kalah Games of Chance [ edit ] Backgammon EinStein würfelt nicht!
